title: A design system that ships to production, not to Figma
subtitle: An API-first design token system that syncs across iOS, Android, web, and Figma from one source of truth
team: Design-led infrastructure, with an engineering partner. Hamburg / Berlin

hero_image.alt: A design system that ships to production, not to Figma — abstract resolution-field cover

sections.headline: A design system that ships to production, not to Figma
sections.subtext: Most design systems are Figma libraries — engineers copy the values into their own platforms by hand. I built a deployed service instead: a Git repo, Style Dictionary, and automated sync, so one token change updates every platform in seconds. Figma is one consumer, not the source.

sections.quote: A design system in Figma is a promise. A design system as an API is a guarantee.
sections.context: The difference between a library designers maintain and a service engineers consume

sections.headline: The problem
sections.content:
Bibel TV was building across four platforms: iOS, Android, web, and connected TV. Each managed its own colors, spacing, and typography — Kotlin values, Swift constants, CSS variables, Figma styles.

When one design decision changed a color, four teams had to update code. In practice, three fell behind, and the platforms drifted apart. The design system was a Figma library that people referenced, with nothing enforcing consistency.

There was also a budget reality: no platform-engineering team to own design infrastructure. If the Head of Design did not build it, no one would.

sections.headline: The architecture
sections.content:
The other options each failed somewhere. A Figma-only library breaks at scale through copy-paste drift. Per-platform token sets give flexibility but make every change an N-times job. A Storybook system handles web and ignores native. Manual sync holds only as long as one person stays perfectly consistent.

What I built is a Git-based token repository that builds to a live API the platforms consume directly.

**One Git repo** holds the tokens, versioned and reviewed like any other code. **A three-tier hierarchy** keeps them sane: global primitives (raw OKLCH colors, spacing, type), semantic tokens (`brand-primary`, `surface-elevated`), and component tokens. **Style Dictionary** builds platform-specific outputs from that single definition. **An automation layer** distributes every committed change to the platform endpoints and the Figma plugin.

images.alt: Design token architecture: a Git repo feeds a Token API that syncs to Android, iOS, web, TV, and Figma — with Figma writing changes back to the repo
images.caption: The architecture: one Git repo builds a Token API that every platform — and Figma — consumes, with edits syncing back.

images.alt: The BibelTV Design Tools Figma plugin syncing tokens from the API, beside the primitive color swatches it generates
images.caption: The same tokens, in Figma — one consumer of the system, kept in sync automatically.

steps.label: Change a token
steps.description: A designer or engineer opens a pull request against the token repo. Standard review, approve, merge.

steps.label: Style Dictionary builds
steps.description: CI runs Style Dictionary, generating platform-specific token files from the single source definition.

steps.label: Sync runs automatically
steps.description: Automation workflows push the built tokens to the platform endpoints and the Figma plugin. No manual step.

steps.label: Platforms pick it up
steps.description: The Android app reads the API on launch and stores tokens locally. Figma updates through the plugin. A token change needs no code deploy.

sections.headline: The decisions that mattered
sections.content:
### The repo is the source, not Figma

This flips the usual dependency. Normally Figma is the design source and each platform keeps its own copy. Here the repo is the source and Figma is a consumer — so design can change without engineering retouching four platforms.

### A standard token format

I used the Design Tokens Community Group format rather than inventing one. New tools will read it natively, and a new hire does not have to learn a proprietary system.

### Design owned the architecture

Token infrastructure is usually platform-engineering work. Owning it meant I could decide the color space, the spacing scale, and the naming semantics without negotiation — but it also meant learning style dictionaries, API endpoints, and automation workflows.

Because the system is a deployed service and not a document, it opens the door to server-driven UI: change a token on the server and every app picks it up on next launch. Adjust a color for a campaign or fix a contrast issue without shipping a build.

metrics.label: Sync
metrics.before: Four teams hand-updating tokens; platforms drifting apart
metrics.after: One commit flows to every platform automatically

metrics.label: Live today
metrics.value: Android consuming tokens via the API; Figma synced automatically through the plugin

metrics.label: Structure
metrics.value: Three-tier token hierarchy — consistency enforced by structure, not by coordination

metrics.label: Future-ready
metrics.value: Architecture supports server-driven UI; iOS and web endpoints defined and ready

sections.headline: What I took from it
sections.content:
Infrastructure work is design leadership work. This design system is not a Figma file, it is a running service — so I had to understand APIs, CI pipelines, and token architecture. Not to become an engineer, but because design decisions embedded in infrastructure carry further than design decisions documented in Figma.

The other lesson: choosing a standard format mattered more than building a clever custom one. It means the system can outlast the person who built it.

executive.summary: Built a deployed design-token system — Git repo, Style Dictionary, automated sync — that keeps Android, Figma, and planned iOS/web in step from one source of truth. Zero manual sync.
executive.angle: Design infrastructure that outlasts the designer who built it

in-depth.summary: Most design systems are Figma libraries. This one is a deployed service. How it was built — token architecture, automation, a path to server-driven UI — without a platform-engineering team.
in-depth.angle: A Head of Design building infrastructure: when to own the token API
